entry.title: Deploying S/4HANA Additional Application Servers on Kubernetes
entry.content:
This blog post is the third part in a series around containerizing SAP S/4HANA systems. I encourage you to read Part 1: Containerizing SAP S/4HANA Systems with Docker and Part 2: Deploying SAP S/4HANA Containers with Kubernetes. In the previous posts, we discussed what Docker is and how we can use it in conjunction with SAP S/4HANA to solve problems related to the N + M landscape. In part 2 we discussed how we can use YAML files to rapidly deploy multiple Kubernetes pods running an image of our S/4HANA system.

Since publishing our first blog post on this topic we have successfully implemented this infrastructure across multiple customer landscapes. Most recently, my colleagues and I wrapped up another project with an innovative customer in the CPG industry, where we successfully deployed additional application servers (AAS) in their QA environment using Kubernetes as a PoC. My colleague Maxim Shmyrev and I have been working hard to make this project successful and we are excited about the potential value it brings to our customers.

What Was the Challenge?

The customer reached out to us and explained that they utilize Infrastructure as Code (IaC) to deploy and manage most of their IT infrastructure. They wanted to rapidly spin up multiple additional application systems for their QA system during periods of high demand.

This is a common scenario for many SAP customers: system usage increases, and Basis teams respond by provisioning additional, costly VMs. They then install the AAS using the Software Provisioning Manager (SWPM) and perform the necessary configurations. Meanwhile, hours pass while the QA backlog continues to grow. By the time the additional capacity is available, project timelines have already been impacted. Once demand increases, additional time must be spent decommissioning these expensive VMs.

Our customer wanted to explore whether deploying their AAS on Azure Kubernetes Services (AKS), instead of provisioning multiple VMs, could provide a more efficient and cost-effective solution.

Our Approach

We created a Docker image of our customer’s additional application server for their QA system using Docker and the Software Provisioning Manager (SWPM. When configuring the YAML files to deploy the pods in AKS we added some additional automation steps at the OS level of the container to ensure a seamless connection to the database and PAS during start-up. During some initial testing we easily deployed 10 additional app servers over two nodes in AKS. Each node has approximately 251 GB of memory allocated and is only using ~35.2 GB (14%) of memory to run these 10 app servers. It should be noted that AKS requires a minimum of two nodes, however, the workload itself could run on a single node if required.

During our cost analysis, we compared the Kubernetes-based deployment against a traditional VM-based approach. A standard E32as v5 virtual machine (32 vCPUs, 256 GiB memory) costs approximately $800 per month. Running 10 additional application servers using traditional VMs would therefore cost approximately $8,000 per month.

During our proof of concept, the total monthly cost for running 10 application servers on Kubernetes was approximately $3,000, including nodes, storage, and supporting infrastructure. The compute nodes themselves accounted for approximately $700 of this total cost.

While this estimate includes additional resources used during the proof of concept, and actual costs may vary depending on configuration and usage, the results indicate potential cost savings in the range of 40–60% when deploying additional application servers on Kubernetes.

The Result

This approach enables our customers to deploy multiple additional application servers within seconds, compared to the best-case scenario of approximately four hours required for manual AAS installation. Furthermore, the additional app servers running in Kubernetes pods can instantly be deleted and the nodes can be scaled down or retired when they are no longer needed. This flexibility allows our customers to remain agile, optimize infrastructure costs, and quickly respond to changing workload demands. If you enjoyed this blog post, please stay tuned for other topics my team members and I will be covering next and be sure to leave a comment below if you have any questions!

entry.title: I reverse‑engineered MM invoice accounting (and here's your new automatic analysis tool!)
entry.content:
I’ve spent the last few years in SAP Support staring at MM invoices that “don’t make sense.”
If you recognize subjects like:

  • “GRIR can not be cleared”

  • “Posting and reversal amount does not match”
  • “Exchange rate difference between GR and IR settling to material account”
  • “Unexpected UMB revaluation posting in MIRO”

…then you already know the kind of stories I’m talking about.

In theory, GR/IR should be simple: goods go in, invoice comes later, the clearing account should end up at zero and reversals should “just mirror” the original. In practice, you add price changes, condition updates, partial goods receipts, provisional/final invoicing, several currencies, and postings across periods – and suddenly the GR/IR line or the reversal document looks like it came from another universe.

For a long time, all we could do would be to share KBAs explaining the standard design and the complex calculations that actually occur in the back-end. This is not efficient, and increases both engineer effort and customer effort.

So I decided to do something slightly extreme: reverse‑engineer the MM invoice accounting logic and encode it into a report that tells the story for you.

The old way

If you’ve ever tried to really understand a tricky MIRO or MR8M document, you’ve probably met some of these notes:

  • 1674290 – Invoice Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA

  • 1609927 – Credit Memo Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 1789924 – Subsequent Debit/Credit Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 3382819 – MR11/MR11SHOW: Calculation logic for GR/IR clearing – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 3039200 – MIRO: KDM and PRD differences – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA

Together, they explain the core clearing logic very well: which quantities and values are used, how WRX is derived, what happens when subsequent debits/credits are handled, what's different in MR11, and how KDM/PRD differences are supposed to look.

The problem is not the documentation; the problem is how much work it takes to apply it:

  • You need to dig out all GR and IR documents for the PO item.

  • You need to consider the right currency context.
  • You need to apply the formulas on top of the customer’s specific history.
  • And you need to do it again for the next incident.

This is exactly where the older IV_WRX_ANALYSIS report was already a big step forward: it automated the WRX clearing calculation and showed that the system was simply following its own rules.

But that approach had some important limits:

  • It focused on WRX only (GR/IR account line).

  • It worked mainly in 1st local currency.
  • It concentrated on the final calculation, not on the whole story behind the document.

At some point I realized: for the kind of questions we see today, “WRX only” is not enough.

What consultants actually ask us

Here is how MM invoice issues usually arrive in Support:

  • “GRIR quantity is equal but PO is open due to currency conversion.”

  • “GRs and IRs imbalance at LC3 level while LC1/LC2 are fine.”
  • “Exchange rate difference between GR and IR is settling to material account; we expected KDM.”
  • “MIRO posting triggers UMB account and we want to know why.”
  • “MR8M: Posting and reversal amount does not match”
  • “New 0‑value line in local currency (LC3) in all VIM invoices after an upgrade.”

Notice what’s going on here:

  • Customers rarely say “Please show me the formula from Note 1674290.”

  • They say “Why is this GR/IR line so strange?” or “Why is this reversal not a mirror?”

All these scenarios have one thing in common:
You can answer them with the existing notes and manual calculations – but only if you’re ready to become a part‑time human calculator.

I wanted something better for consultants: a way to get the same level of explanation without having to rebuild all equations every time.

What I decided to build instead

Therefore, it is not enough to show to the consultants how WRX was calculated. It would be much better to access the whole story behind the invoice:

  • Which documents were taken into account.

  • How quantities and values accumulated over time.
  • How the system split the outcome into stock (BSX), price differences (PRD), exchange‑rate differences (KDM), GR/IR clearing (WRX), and sometimes revaluation (UMB) and other lines.
  • And, specially, why the accounting document is following standard design and, even if the amounts look strange, the system knows it did the right thing

So I built a new analysis report with a very simple idea: If you’re going to trust the accounting document, let’s show you exactly what the system saw and how it used it.

Design goals were straightforward:

  1. Show the entire PO/SA history for the invoice item
    Not just a final WRX total, but a line‑by‑line list of relevant GRs and IRs, so it’s obvious which documents the system actually considered.

  2. Support multiple currencies
    Consultants can switch the report to document currency or up to three local currencies, which is crucial when GR/IR appears balanced in one currency but strange in another.
  3. Explain the logic, not just print numbers
    After the history tables, the report has a “brain” section that walks through the main steps of the posting logic – in a way that matches the KBAs, but applied to the specific case you’re analyzing.
  4. Keep the input simple
    On the selection screen you basically tell the report:
    • which invoice item to analyze,
    • which currency type you want to see, and
    • in case of multiple account assignment, which account assignment line.

  5. Make it easy to share and debug
    When a customer sends the report output back to SAP, the structure is friendly both for humans and for any future tooling that needs to understand what happened.

And because the older IV_WRX_ANALYSIS report was already useful, I didn’t throw it away: I used it as a basis for this new solution.

What the new report actually shows

Without going into technical field names, here’s how the output is organized and why it helps.

Part 1: The ingredients

  1. Context block – what are we analyzing?
    At the top you see the invoice number, fiscal year, item, the PO reference and the general context.

  2. Goods Receipt history – “how did we get here on the GR side?”
    The first table lists all relevant GR documents for that PO item, and explains how GR quantities and amounts contribute to the final totals.

  3. Invoice history – “what happened before this invoice?”
    The next table does the same for previous IRs (and subsequent debits/credits), including reversals.
    It shows how much has already been invoiced before the document you’re analyzing.

Part 2: The Recipe

  1. Logic explanation 
    This is the part that replaces pages of manual calculation:

    • It shows how the GR/IR clearing line is derived, so you can see why WRX doesn’t always match the item amount.

    • It explains how the system splits the amount into:

      • Stock postings (BSX),
      • Price differences (PRD),
      • Exchange‑rate differences (KDM),
      • and, when relevant, revaluation (UMB) and other lines such as EIN, EKG, FRE etc.

    You don’t have to know every detail of the formulas from the KBAs – the report is essentially doing those calculations for you and showing the intermediate steps.

Part 3: The results

Final amounts – “what should the FI document look like?”
At the end, the report prints the final calculated amounts per transaction key, in a format that mirrors the accounting document.
This is the part you can compare directly with the FI document. If they match, you’ve just proved that the system is behaving as designed (and you didn’t need a spreadsheet to get there).

Your turn

The report is intentionally not a monster transaction. It’s something you can run quickly whenever a document is questioned and you need to audit results and investigate documents that look wrong.

To keep things simple (and consistent with SAP Support processes), the report ZIV_ACCOUNTING_ANALYSIS is delivered via SAP Note 2427297 – Analysis report to explain IV accounting documents

From there you can:

  • Download the report source from the attachments of the note and implement it manually via SE38 in your system, as described in the note.

    • The report is not available for SNOTE implementation. For that reason, you need to get the attachment for manual implementation
  • Then run it like any other custom analysis tool, using the straightforward selection screen.

The note itself remains the official reference for obtaining and implementing the report; the blog post you’re reading is about why it exists and how it can help you as a consultant.

entry.title: *BCK - The Silver Lining : Rescuing Your FBL1N Selection Screen in S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Introduction - During the SAP ECC era, index tables like BSIK, BSAK, BSAD, BSID etc. were transparent tables, making them straightforward to extend directly. However, in S/4HANA, the architecture changed. These tables have been converted into Core Data Services (CDS) views built on top of BKPF and BSEG. Because of various system constraints (outlined in SAP Note 3170581), developers can no longer extend these views directly.

So, how do we move forward?

*The Silver Lining:  With the introduction of S/4HANA, SAP provided additional tables such as BSIS_BCK, BSAS_BCK, BSIK_BCK, and BSAK_BCK (Accounting: Secondary Index for G/L, Vendor, Customer Accounts - Back-up)

These are part of the Compatibility Views and are technical structures primarily used for data migration and archiving from ECC systems. While new S/4HANA postings do not update these specific tables, the core CDS views (like BSIK and BSAK) are linked to them. This linkage is the key to our solution: if we can add new fields to these *BCK tables, the fields will subsequently reflect in the core CDS views

Prerequisites for Extension -

  1. The extended fields you want to add must already exist in either the BKPF or BSEG tables.
  2. Both the open items table (BSIK_BCK) and the cleared items table (BSAK_BCK) must be extended with the exact same fields in the exact same order.

Step-by-Step Technical Guide ( Environment - S/4 HANA OP 2025 )

Step 1: Append Structures to the Backup Tables First, you need to create an append structure to Table BSIK_BCK. Then, repeat this process by adding an identical append structure to Table BSAK_BCK.

 

Append structure to Table BSAK_BCK

 

Note: Simply appending the structure to the *BCK tables will not immediately extend the BSIK view.

BSIK is not extended yet

Step 2: Regenerate the CDS Views To push the newly appended fields into the main views, you must execute a specific report.

Run the ABAP report: FGL_MIG_REGENERATE_CDS_GL.

Executing this report will regenerate the views of the index tables (BSIK, BSAK, BSAD, BSID, etc.) and successfully add the new fields from the BSIK_BCK append structure into the main BSIK view.

The fields have been added to the view BSIK

Step 3: Update the Logical Database for FBL1N Now that the views are extended, the final phase is to expose these fields on the FBL1N selection screen, referencing SAP Note 3455167.

Go to transaction SE36.

Select Logical Database KDF.

Navigate to Extras -> Selection View, and choose Copy to CUS (Customer space).

Add Group 04 and label it as 'Additional Selection'.

Assign Group ID 04 to your newly extended fields.

Once these steps are completed and saved, your custom fields will successfully appear in the FBL1N selection screen, ready for business users to leverage.

  • The same concept should work across supplier, customer and GL line report ( FBL1N, FBL5N, FBL3N ).
  • While SAP recommends to use equivalent Fiori app (F0712 – Manage supplier line item in this case) which can be extended using ‘custom fields and logic’ app, there is still wide usage of FBL1N among business users.

Conclusion - The underlying business requirement to enhance FBL1N screen remains a driver for business user to work efficiently and same is achieved in S/4 using the steps mentioned in this blog post.

entry.title: SAP Green Ledger: Q1-26 Release Updates and Highlights
entry.content:
With this blog post, I would like to introduce a regular quarterly update series highlighting the latest innovations and enhancements in SAP Green Ledger. These updates provide an overview of new features and improvements delivered during the last quarter, helping you stay up to date with ongoing developments.

Short introduction: What is SAP Green Ledger? SAP Green Ledger supports organizations in integrating carbon into financial processes. It runs as an application on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and integrates with SAP S/4HANA Cloud–based finance systems. It enables organizations to monitor and document carbon emission impacts at transaction level and link emissions to financial objects. By using established financial structures such as accounts, company codes, and controlling objects, carbon emissions can be consistently assigned to business activities and financial categories. This supports transparent and auditable reporting, as well as data-driven operational and strategic decision-making.

With Q1 2026, SAP Green Ledger continues to evolve, supporting organizations in moving beyond emissions tracking toward managing carbon as a financial dimension. The features released in the first quarter of this year bring a range of enhancements in carbon liability management, regulatory readiness, and financial integration, along with improvements that make everyday processes more transparent and flexible. This blog post covers the following new features:

  • CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities
  • Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates
  • Linking Carbon Data to Projects
  • Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems
  • Posting on Blocked Accounts
  • Reversing Manual Journal Entries
  • Further Updates and Improvements

 

CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities

One of the key highlights this quarter is the support for carbon certificate data and carbon liabilities. Carbon certificates and liabilities play a central role in regulatory frameworks such as the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), where companies must cover embedded emissions with certificates that carry a financial value. Since the EU CBAM came into effect in January 2026, companies that import specific goods into the EU are now required to report embedded emissions in these imported goods and build up corresponding carbon liabilities. While the actual purchase of EU CBAM certificates will only begin in 2027, organizations already need to track and value these obligations today.

To support these new requirements, the new Configure Carbon Certificate Data app provides a centralized entry point for maintaining the data required for carbon certificate handling and valuation. You can create and maintain Emissions Trading System (ETS) prices, which represent the cost of emitting one metric ton of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e). Since these prices fluctuate over time and directly impact liability valuation, maintaining them each calendar week helps ensure accuracy and alignment with market developments.

Maintaining Weekly EU ETS Prices (Illustrative Values Only)

In addition, you can display available company codes and access yearly CBAM factors. These factors determine the portion of embedded emissions that must be covered by CBAM certificates. By bringing ETS pricing, company context, and CBAM factors together, the app creates a consistent foundation that connects configuration with carbon data collection, liability calculation, and journal entry processing.

The flow is further supported by enhancements in the Manage Carbon Collections app. When creating a carbon collection, you can now indicate whether carbon certificates are required and maintain relevant information such as the number of certificates to surrender. This ensures that certificate requirements are captured consistently at the point of data entry. The information can then be used in later processing steps.

Carbon Certificate -relevant Carbon Collection

Building on this, the new Manage Carbon Liabilities app provides a dedicated space to view and evaluate carbon liabilities resulting from carbon collections that require certificates. You can analyze both the number of certificates to surrender as well as their corresponding monetary value based on ETS prices, reflecting the financial obligations associated with embedded emissions.

A key part of this process is fair value calculation. The system now calculates the fair value of carbon liabilities automatically for the current calendar year. This calculation is triggered whenever ETS prices are created, updated, or deleted, as well as when carbon collections requiring certificates are posted to the journal. Fair values are calculated by multiplying the number of certificates to surrender by the latest quarterly average ETS price. The calculated fair value covers the period from January 1 to the selected posting date within the year. At the same time, you can still trigger fair value calculations manually. This allows you to recalculate values when needed while keeping posting logic consistent.

Transparency is further improved through integration with journal entries as in the Manage Journal Entries app, you can review data originating from carbon collections. This includes, next to the line items for the collected GHG emissions, CBAM embedded emissions and certificate quantities now.

Journal Entries for CBAM-relevant Carbon Collection

Together, these capabilities provide greater transparency and control over how carbon liabilities are valued and reflected in financial processes. For more information, check SAP Help: Carbon Certificate Management. Looking ahead, we will continue to further develop support in this area. While the current focus is on supporting the calculation and management of carbon liabilities, future developments will address the next phase of EU CBAM. Starting from 2027, companies will be required to purchase CBAM certificates to cover their reported emissions. To support this, SAP Green Ledger will be extended with dedicated certificate management capabilities.

 

Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates

To support CBAM-relevant journal entry postings, account determination for carbon certificates has been enhanced with the Determine Mandatory Accounts app (formerly Manage Carbon-Only Accounts). It enables you to assign specific CBAM accounts to charts of accounts and company codes, supporting transactions such as liability recognition and fair value adjustments. This setup is mandatory for organizations subject to EU CBAM requirements. The app provides predefined CBAM account types for assets, liabilities, and operating expenses, which are typically maintained in a connected finance system and used in SAP Green Ledger. Postings to these accounts are generated automatically, ensuring alignment with underlying carbon processes.

Accounts Assignment for EU CBAM Accounts

Check out SAP Help for more information: Assign Carbon Certificate Accounts.

 

Linking Carbon Data to Projects

Carbon data can now be linked to projects and WBS (work breakdown structure) elements. A new WBS element field is available across the apps Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, Display Account Balances, and the Carbon Data Dashboard. This allows you to link carbon emissions directly to specific WBS elements. For that, project and WBS master data can now be imported from a connected finance system using our master data import capabilities and viewed in the new Display Projects app. This enhancement enables you to gain a more structured and detailed view of carbon data and makes it possible to analyze emissions at the level of individual projects and WBS elements.

Example Project in the Display Projects App

 

Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems

Another improvement is the increased flexibility in system integration with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management systems. Before, you could only connect one business system with the role SAP Sustainability Footprint Management to a SAP Green Ledger system. Now it is possible to create multiple business systems with that role. This allows you to work with more than one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system, for example when importing carbon data from different sources. At the same time, each finance business system can be associated with only one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system connected to a SAP Green Ledger system. This clear assignment ensures consistent and well-structured data integration. The setup is handled through business system configuration, where you define the system, assign its role, and maintain integration details such as the connected finance system.

 

Posting on Blocked Accounts

Posting behaviour has been made more flexible in SAP Green Ledger. Carbon collections can now be posted on accounts even if they are currently marked as blocked in the system. This is particularly relevant in scenarios where the account status may differ between SAP Green Ledger and the connected finance system. This can occur because account data in SAP Green Ledger is not updated in real time but is instead based on data imports from the finance system. As a result, the status in SAP Green Ledger may not always reflect the most current state.

Warning Message when Posting on a Blocked Account

Against this background, postings can still be carried out even if an account is indicated as blocked in SAP Green Ledger at a given point in time. Before posting, a warning message informs you if the account is marked as blocked. To ensure accuracy, you can verify the actual status of the account directly in the connected finance business system. Overall, this adjustment allows you to continue processes without interruption while still maintaining transparency and control over account usage.

 

Reversing Manual Journal Entries

You can now reverse manual journal entries in our Manage Journal Entries app, which makes it easier to correct postings and maintain accurate carbon data records. Reversing journal entries is possible if certain conditions are met, such as having the appropriate source type and not having been reversed previously. Once reversed, the system automatically creates corresponding journal entries in which debits and credits are swapped, ensuring that the financial impact is fully offset while preserving traceability.

The reversal information, including status, who initiated the reversal and when as well as the reversed journal entries, is available in the journal entry document, allowing you to track changes and maintain compliance with accounting standards. This enhancement helps ensure data accuracy while supporting controlled correction processes within SAP Green Ledger. For more details, have a look at SAP Help: Reverse Journal Entries.

 

Further Updates and Improvements

In addition to the key capabilities described above, several additional enhancements have been introduced to improve usability and transparency:

  • A new Journal Entry Source Type field is now available in key apps such as Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, and Display Account Balances and provides visibility into how journal entries were created (e.g., through allocation processes, liability valuation, carbon collection postings, imports, or manual entries and reversals).
  • Updates to the allocation functionality include the renaming of the allocation type Manual Allocation to Periodic Allocation and related UI adjustments.
  • Certain allocation options have been streamlined, such as the removal of the allocation type Account Balance Allocation from the Display Account Balances app.
  • External commenting is now available in the User Guide, enabling direct feedback and collaboration, see also Blog Post.

 

Additional Information

If you want to dive deeper into SAP Green Ledger, the SAP Help Portal is a good place to start. It provides detailed information on concepts, configuration, and application usage. You can also check the What’s New section, which is updated with every release and gives a complete overview of the latest additions. To see what's coming next, the SAP Road Map Explorer offers insight into planned features and upcoming innovations. It is updated regularly and outlines how the solution will continue to evolve.

entry.title: Від ECC до S/4HANA: що важливо знати до 2027 року.
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA — один із найзатребуваніших напрямів в SAP-екосистемі. Багато компаній вже розпочали або планують перехід, проте для більшості фахівців та керівників залишається відкритим питання: що це насправді за система, чим вона принципово відрізняється від SAP ECC і що означає цей перехід на практиці. Щоб внести ясність, зібрали ключові моменти детального матеріалу — від базових понять до практичних аспектів міграції. 

 

Чому це актуально саме зараз? 

Перехід на SAP S/4HANA стає критично важливим, оскільки 2027 року закінчується основна підтримка SAP ERP (Business Suite). У 2027 році, компанії, що працюють на SAP ECC, більше не отримуватимуть оновлень безпеки, виправлень помилок, підтримки відповідності та аудиту, а також технічної підтримки від SAP.

 

Що таке SAP S/4HANA простими словами?

Це сучасна ERP-система на базі in-memory бази даних, яка об'єднує фінанси, HR, продажі, виробництво та логістику в одному місці.

Головна перевага — обробка та аналіз великих обсягів даних у реальному часі, що забезпечує значно вищу продуктивність порівняно з традиційними системами.

Доступна у двох варіантах: Cloud (публічний та приватний) та On-Premise.

 

Три причини мігрувати з SAP ECC до SAP S/4HANA:

  • Кібербезпека та відповідність вимогам (GDPR та ін.)
  • Несумісність із сучасними технологіями: AI, IoT, хмара
  • Конкурентне відставання без аналітики в реальному часі

👉Детальне порівняння

 

Можливості SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA допомагає приймати рішення швидше та працювати ефективніше. Ключові можливості:

  • Аналітика та звітність у реальному часі
  • AI/ML: прогнозування попиту, виявлення шахрайства, предиктивне обслуговування обладнання, аналіз клієнтів
  • Сучасний інтерфейс SAP Fiori
  • Автоматизація процесів та спрощена модель даних (звіти — за 2 секунди замість 30 хвилин)
  • Оптимізація ланцюга постачання
  • Хмарна масштабованість та інтеграція із зовнішніми системами

Різниця між SAP ECC та S/4HANA найкраще видна на конкретному прикладі — від фази отримання замовлення до фактичної оплати.

👉Детальна таблиця

 

Моделі впровадження SAP S/4HANA

Компанії можуть впровадити SAP S/4HANA по-різному — залежно від бізнес-потреб, рівня кастомізації та IT-стратегії.

Три основні варіанти:

  • Public Cloud (GROW)
  • Private Cloud (RISE)
  • On-Premise

👉Про моделі впровадження і різницю

 

Вартість SAP S/4HANA

Фіксованої ціни немає — вартість залежить від кількості користувачів, моделі впровадження (cloud/on-premise), необхідних модулів та складності проєкту.

Загалом хмарний варіант передбачає нижчу початкову вартість, інфраструктуру включену у підписку та швидше впровадження. On-Premise потребує більших початкових інвестицій і вищого TCO у довгостроковій перспективі.

👉Порівняння вартості Cloud vs On-Premise

 

Навчання та сертифікація

SAP S/4HANA потребує часу на вивчення, але опанувати її цілком реально. Залежно від обраного напряму, сертифікація займає від 2 тижнів до 2 місяців. Для старту доступні: SAP Young Professionals Program — навчання із сертифікацією та входом в SAP-екосистему, або самостійні курси на платформі SAP Learning.

 

Ознайомтеся з деталями у повній статті від Valentina Guevara та João Simões -  "5 Things You Need to Know Before Starting with SAP S/4HANA"

entry.title: Managing Content Change Notification Issues During SAP Fiori Launchpad Content Exposure to SAP BTP
entry.content:
With the SAP Fiori launchpad content exposure, you can integrate launchpad content from a product into SAP BTP by using an automatic synchronization. During the automatic synchronization, it may happen that the exposure log receives the following error message “Notification for content channel ‘xyz’ not sent. Manual fetch required” (Message Class: /UI2/CDM3_EXP, Message Number: 399).

As you may not immediately recognize that this issue happened, this blog post provides a way of how you can monitor this type of error message using email notification from SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (SAP Cloud ALM).

In short, in the SAP Cloud ALM Integration & Exception Monitoring app, you do the following:

  1. Enable the ABAP application log for the system          
  2. Add a filter for the error message
  3. Add a notification for the error

As an example, we use an SAP S/4HANA system.

Disclaimer: This article is written from the viewpoint of a consumer of SAP Cloud ALM. If you have SAP Cloud ALM-specific questions, such as how to set up your system in SAP Cloud ALM, please reach out to SAP Cloud ALM directly.

Check the following SAP Help Portal pages to see whether you are affected by this issue:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

  • Enable Content Change Notifications
  • Display Launchpad Content Exposure Logs

SAP Integrated Business Planning

  • Enable Content Change Notifications
  • Display Launchpad Content Exposure Logs

SAP S/4HANA on-premise

  • Manage Content Change Notifications
  • Launchpad Content Exposure Troubleshooting - Error Analysis   

Prerequisites

Before we can start with the parts specific to launchpad exposure in the SAP Cloud ALM configuration, you set up your system in SAP Cloud ALM.

In addition, please ensure that you have the Integration Architect role. See SAP Cloud ALM overview page.

Enabling ABAP Application Log for Your System

First, you configure your system to collect data for the ABAP Application Log.

  1. In SAP Cloud ALM, in the Operations space, open the Integration & Exception Monitoring app.
  2. Now, let’s add a system tile to the overview: In the upper right corner, choose the Select a Scope icon and filter for your system. Select it in the table and choose Apply.
  3. On the overview page, choose the system tile and from its menu, choose Edit Configuration.
  4. For your system, toggle the Data Collection to On.
  5. For your system, choose the arrow icon to go to the details page.
  6. Activate the ABAP Application Log entry.

Adding a Filter for the Launchpad Content Exposure Error Message

Now, let’s create a filter so that we can monitor when the error message “Notification for content channel ‘xyz’ not sent. Manual fetch required” is written to the ABAP Application Log.

  1. In the same screen as above, for the ABAP Application Log, enter the monitoring and events view.
  2. To add the filter, in the Filters table, choose Add.
  3. Enter a filter name. Then, enter the following filter parameters:

    Parameter

    Operator

    Value

    Object

    Is

    /UI2/BE

    Subobject

    Is

    /UI2/CDM3

    Message ID

    Is

    /UI2/CDM3_EXP

    Message Number

    Is

    399

    Status

    Is

    E


  4. Save the filter by choosing the Save button at the bottom of the dialog.

Adding an Event to Be Notified About an Error

The last step is to create an event so that you get notified once the filter which we have created in the previous step detects the launchpad content exposure error message.

Please note that this event will not only be initiated for the launchpad content exposure error message. If you had configured other ABAP Application Log filters in addition, the event will be initiated as well. The reason is that on the date of publication of this blog post, according to 3452860 - How to setup filters for the category "ABAP application log" in Exception Monitoring of SAP Cloud ALM, it is not possible to add filters to the event itself.

  1. To create the event, navigate to the Events tab and choose Add.
  2. As Event Name, choose Erroneous ABAP Application Log and enter a display name.
  3. Define the actions: Activate Create Alert. For example, you can also configure the event to send an email once there is an error written into the ABAP Application Log.
     
  4. Save your entries.

Checking the Event

In the Integration & Exception Monitoring app, you can view all events that you have created and that were initiated on the Alerting tab.

  1. Choose the Alerting tab.
  2. Once the launchpad content exposure error happens, you can see it in the table

Conclusion:

This blog post covered how to proactively monitor HTTP 401 content change notification failures during SAP Fiori launchpad content exposure to SAP BTP using SAP Cloud ALM's Integration & Exception Monitoring app. You have learned how to enable ABAP Application Log data collection, create a targeted filter for message /UI2/CDM3_EXP 399, and configure automated email notifications to detect synchronization issues before they impact your users. By implementing this monitoring approach, you can transform reactive troubleshooting into proactive incident management, ensuring continuous content availability between your on-premise systems and SAP BTP.

If you have questions about the configuration steps or would like to share your experiences with launchpad content exposure monitoring, please feel free to leave a comment below.

entry.title: Universal Parallel Accounting - Hands on in S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition 2023
entry.content:
UPA is the natural evolution of different SAP ERP functionalities, like for example Costing-Based COPA, Margin Analysis, COGM, Currency and Valuation profiles. UPA brings the goal of having different parallel views  under the lean finance philosophy: simple and efficient processes,  in a single source of truth, easy to consume for business users. 

Activate UPA and unlock management accounting that’s fully integrated into compliance: post different parallel values per ledger across the entire value chain to deliver simultaneous Group and Local GAAP reporting, reduce reconciliation effort, and accelerate complex multi-GAAP implementations.

We are a group of Finance SAP Consultants with different competencies in different fields, we decided to come together to test UPA, following same steps as a new customer would do, putting ourselves in our customer' shoes. We adopted the green field approach, so after obtaining a new system we installed the UPA Business Function and we implemented the SAP UPA best practices.

We would like to concentrate on a "hands on" exercise executed on an S/4HANA 2023 internal system. Please be aware that UPA is available for new Implementation customers from S/4HANA 2022 and from S/4HANA 2023 for Customers having systems with existing data.

BUSINESS CASE 

There are 4 Use cases addressed by Universal Parallel Accounting.

In this article we will concentrate on the first one: Parallel Valuations- Unconsolidated view for International companies which need to report Group and Local GAAP.  

UPA use cases

“Two different ledgers and accounting principles used consistently across the value chain”

(definition by Janet Salmon, chief product owner for SAP Management Accounting).

 

When UPA is active, it is possible to write parallel different values per ledger across the entire value chain. This gives the possibility of being compliant with different accounting principles in the management accounting as well to obtain a complete GAAP financial statement.    

UPA Value Chain

Even  If UPA is not implemented, parallel accounting is still reachable across the value chain but can only copy, in some specific part of the value chain,  the values originally written in the Leading ledger. In these processes the leading ledger value is applied to all ledgers. This may causes need for manual adjustments and/or additional revaluation activities at month-end. 

Value Chain without UPA

The aim of this blog is to analyze each box of the UPA Value Chain in sequence and to demonstrate the results of parallel ledger posting in an unconsolidated view. 

The business scenario described below concerns a company (belonging to a Group of companies) which want to track impact of the different transactional flows (from posting overhead costs  to asset capitalization, depreciation,  to consumption of activities and materials for the production and sales of a finished product), in the Financial statement items (revenues, Investments , Asset Under Constructions, Final Assets, Overheads, Cost of Goods Manufactured, Cost of Goods Sold , Sales Revenues and Margins),  evaluated following different accounting principles. 

 

FUNCTIONAL TESTS

Find below the previous value chain adapted with the functional steps of our functional tests. 

Use Cases Addressed by UPA

In the Best Practices we activated we had Controlling Area Currency Dollar (USD) and Company Code Currency Euro (EUR). So every posting we will have in this functional test  will be in both currencies. Only when entering the cost rate is it managed in a single currency type (the controlling area currency in this example)

So let's start with the first step: Cost posted to the investment internal order.  

1. Posting  a Finance Document on an Internal Order 

Primary cost to Internal Order

 

SCOPE ITEM

J54 Post primary costs on the internal order

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger common manual posting to internal order.

Business Case Coverage

Capitalizable expenses allowed as part of the assets cost, depending on the accounting principle.

For example, financial expenses from a loan related to a short-term construction project: the adjustment may only apply in one ledger (Local GAAP but not IFRS)

With UPA/Without UPA

No differences with installation where UPA is not active. We also decided to use the same values for the 2 ledgers

Please be aware that we decided to use internal orders instead of WBS elements because we wanted to test the internal orders that are still available in the S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition.

F0717A Manage Journal Entries-1

 

F0717A Manage Journal Entries-2

We decided to post the same values in Ledger 0L and 2L (by using ledger group 0L2L). 

F4023 Display Line Items 0L Primary Cost

F4023 Display Line Items 2L Primary Cost

2. Manage Cost Rate Plan

Activity Allocations

We planned here different rates per ledger on both activity types (Setup and Personnel Hours) for brevity we used the same activity types for activity type allocation to an Internal order (next step) and later for Product cost planning calculation. Please note that the controlling area currency used is USD.

F3162 Manage Cost Rates-Plan 0L and 2L

3. Direct Activity Allocation 

Activity Allocations II

SCOPE ITEM

J54 Direct Activity Allocation

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger-specific valuation of activity consumption

Business Case Coverage

Activity allocation (e.g. consulting activities) previously valuated with different prices per ledger are now allocated from the cost center to the Internal Order resulting in different amounts posted by ledger (in order to accommodate local market differences or/and local inventory- asset valuation rules)

With UPA/Without UPA

Activity allocations always post to all ledgers (with or without UPA)

Only with UPA the values posted can differ between ledgers (because of different activity cost rates).

Without UPA, the system uses the ledger-independent cost rate.

See  below the accounting document showing activity allocation from the cost center to the internal order (10 hours per activity type):

F3697 Manage Direct Activity Allocation

Note that there is a single journal entry with postings to two ledgers, based on the reference document 300000300 posted in the previous step.

F4023 Display Line Items 0L

F4023 Display Line Items 2L

As the activity price is 30 USD/hour in ledger 0L and 50 USD/hour in ledger 2L, different amounts are posted per ledger. You can see here that the Activity Allocation From the Cost Center to the internal Order has different values per Ledger.  

4. Settlement- Asset Under Construction  

Settlement AuC

SCOPE ITEM

6DT Asset Under Construction

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger Specific settlement rules  for WBS/ Internal Order according to different accounting principles.

Business Case Coverage

Asset Under Construction capitalization values differs by country based on local GAAP vs. IFRS requirements.

With UPA/Without UPA

UPA allows different settlement rules per ledger.

With UPA, AUC settlement is based on the combination of company code and ledger (FI, CO and AM are ledger-based areas)

Without UPA, settlement values are the same for all ledgers (it´s not an end-to-end  process)

It´s not possible to use UPA without IAA or UPA in connection with “New Asset Accounting”

You can manage different settlement rules per ledger. To simplify this example, the settlement rules in the order are assumed to be identical across all ledgers, as you can see in the image below:

F5695 Manage Setllement Rules-Internal Orders

However, keep in mind that different settlement amounts in the receiver can result from:

  • Ledger-specific settlement rules in the sender object

  • Ledger-specific posted amounts in the sender object

In our case, different settlement results are obtained per ledger due to different posted amounts. 

In the image below, you can see how UPA remains consistent throughout the entire asset lifecycle:

Asset capitalization and depreciation are ledger-specific in UPA, no manual adjustments or clearing are required, ensuring a seamless asset lifecycle (see image below).

Asset Accounting with-without UPA

When UPA is activated, the option for ledger-specific distribution rules is enabled in the configuration activities for the settlement profile.

Chart of depreciation is only used as an object for depreciation key management.

Depreciation areas for parallel currencies are no longer required; each area is automatically managed in all currencies of its assigned ledger.

Capitalization 

Asset Capitalization

 

SCOPE ITEM

J62 Intelligent Asset Accounting

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger Specific settlement rules  for WBS/ order and depreciation run for assets according to different accounting principles.

Business Case Coverage

Asset capitalization and depreciation values differs by country based on local GAAP vs. IFRS requirements.

With UPA/Without UPA

UPA allows different settlement rules per ledger.

With UPA, asset depreciation calculation is based on the combination of company code and ledger (FI, CO and AM are ledger-based areas)

Without UPA, settlement and depreciation values are the same for all ledgers (It´s not an end-to-end  process)

It´s not possible to use UPA without IAA or UPA in connection with “New Asset Accounting”

F0717A Manage Journal Entries 0L

F0717A Manage Journal Entries 2L

As you can see, up to this point we have been working with two currency types: EUR and USD mentioned at the very top of this blog. To simplify the next steps, we will stick to currency type EUR for the Fixed Asset analysis, that results in the following amounts:

F0717A Manage Journal Entries 0L EUR

F0717A Manage Journal Entries 2L EUR

F1684 Manage Fixed Assets

Capitalization (AuC -->Fixed Asset)

The AuC 40000001-0 is settled to Final Fixed Asset 20000000-0.

F3425 Manage Fixed Assets - Final Asset with Reference to AuC 0L

5. Post Depreciation

Asset Depreciation

SCOPE ITEM

J62 Intelligent Asset Accounting

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger Specific settlement rules  for WBS/ order and depreciation run for assets according to different accounting principles.

With UPA/Without UPA

Without UPA, settlement simply takes the values from the leading ledger and copies them into ledger 2L or any other additional ledgers.

This resulted in balances sitting on order/WBS A or order/WBS B that needed to be cleared manually, and they were not representative. The value of the asset under construction in ledger 2L was therefore not reliable at all.

Let’s review the ledger-specific capitalization amounts for assets resulting from internal order settlement to the AuC 40000001-0:

F3425 Manage Fixed Assets- Ledger Specific Postings AuC 0L.

F3425 Manage Fixed Assets- Ledger Specific Postings AuC 2L.

The final asset’s capitalization and depreciation values are ledger-specific:

F3425 Final Asset Capitalization and depreciation ledger specific postings 0L

F3425 Final Asset Capitalization and depreciation ledger specific postings 2L

Once again, the analysis remains available in parallel currencies for both ledgers, as shown in the image below:

F3425 Final Asset Capitalization and depreciation ledger specific postings USD

6. Universal Allocation

Universal Allocation

SCOPE ITEM

J54 Universal Allocation

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger specific Automatic Allocation between Cost Centers

Business Case Coverage

Automatic Allocations of costs per ledger. In this specific case we decided to use the same rules per every ledger .

With UPA/Without UPA

Same result between UPA and S/4HANA (Universal allocation is already able to execute allocation by ledger in S/4HANA). In UPA the only way to distribute cost is the universal allocation. As in S/4HANA Universal Allocation  supports Ledger allocations and 10 currencies.

As said, the Universal allocation functionality is already working per single ledger even without UPA. So to show you how it works we inserted here an exercise but you can execute it in the same way in a not UPA environment. 

In the following picture you can see the allocation flow from cost center 10101101 to 101013012 and 10101301

F4022 Allocation Flow

F4363 Allocation Result Ledger

F4363 Allocation Result 0L Sender

F4363 Allocation Result 0L Receiver

 

F4363 Allocation Result 2L Sender

F4363 Allocation Result 2L Receiver

 7. Standard Cost Calculation

Standard Cost Calculation

 

SCOPE ITEM

BEG Standard Cost Calculation

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Multiple material valuation per ledger and legal view.

Business Case Coverage

Different accounting principles for inventory valuation, depending on local/ global compliance requirements.

With UPA/Without UPA

With UPA multiple cost estimates might be created (in reference to specific ledger costing variant) in order to update the various ledger. The material may be valuated with different inventory price per ledger (a single price can also be used when the cost variant is not assigned to a ledger- ledger field blank). These different prices are available for the full end-to-end flow (with different COGS and contribution margins per ledger)

Without UPA, none of the solutions provided a full E2E process, because they weren’t defined by ledger, but by other parameters.

Parallel accounting enables you to calculate the cost of goods manufactured based on various accounting principles. The underlying accounting principle is assigned to each ledger. By using ledger-specific costing variants, costing types and valuation variants, you can calculate different material prices per ledger. Please check the configuration in your system.

The costing type 2L (linked to the costing variant P02L) is assigned to ledger 2L. It means the costing variant P02L will update only ledger 2L with the results of the material cost estimate, which is expected to be different from Ledger 0L.

The product master data valuation tab contains information about inventory prices across all ledgers and currencies, as shown in the image below:

F1602 Manage Product Master Data Ledgers and Currencies

Likewise, the material stocks are valuated accordingly:

F2680 Manage Material Valuation

After releasing the material cost estimate, inventory prices will be updated per ledger. Stock movements will be valuated differently in each ledger if different prices exist.

It’s not just materials that can have different ledger prices; cost rates for activities in a cost center can vary by ledger too (as seen before). For example, depreciation rates often differ by country, so the the activity "machine hours" can carry different values depending on the ledger. 

Let’s take a look at the following example of direct labor and production setup costs (activity types: 11 and 3):

F3162 Manage Cost Rates-Plan

During a costing run, SAP reads activity cost rates for activity types 3 and 11 according to the ledger assigned to each costing variant. In the example, P00L (ledger 0L) picks up the rates in the red box while P02L (ledger 2L) picks up the rates in the blue box. This setup lets you model country-specific direct labor and production setup costs by ledger automatically, without manual adjustments.

F1865 Manage Costing Runs-P00L

F1865 Manage Costing Runs-P02L

During the release step, the system updates the material prices and creates the corresponding posting documents. The stock revaluation is calculated as:

(new standard price – previous standard price) × stock quantity

resulting in different amounts per ledger and currency type, as shown below.

F0717A Manage Journal Entries-0L

F0717A Manage Journal Entries-2L

8. Product Cost Posting

Production Cost Posting

 

SCOPE ITEM

2UG  3F0 Production Process by ledger event-based

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger-specific WIP and variance calculation or/and optional manual execution.

Business Case Coverage

End-to-end integration between FI and CO with parallel valuation (assets, activities, inventory) enabling multiple accounting principles and extended to CO production processes (WIP and variance settlement)

With UPA/Without UPA

UPA enables valuation of GI/GR and activity consumption at IFRS vs local GAAP rates and also supports ledger-specific settlement of actual production variances.

UPA is only working with the event-based approach (3F0)

Without UPA order settlement (WIP/variances) simply takes the values in the leading ledger and copying them into whatever other additional ledgers.

Let’s go through the production process, starting from the final picture (the calculated actual unit prices) and trace back the path by which those prices are determined for ledger 0L and 2L. This latest calculation is explained further in the blog, in the Actual Costing section.

For simplicity, we will focus on currency type EUR in the Material Price Analysis app, although cross-currency types analysis per ledger is supported.

CKM3N Material Price Analysis- Ledger 0L

CKM3N Material Price Analysis- Ledger 2L

Goods issues (GIs) posted at production order confirmation (using ledger-specific material prices and activity allocations based on each ledger’s cost rates ) may debit different amounts to the order in each ledger:

F3365 Display Document Flow- Order Confirmation GI

You can see the finance postings of material documents 4900000031 and 4900000032 for both ledgers in the image below. Note that, in this case, raw materials do not use different inventory prices per ledger; however, the information is still posted by ledger:

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Amounts debited to the production order 0L

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Amounts debited to the production order 2L

Goods receipts (GR) posted at production order confirmation (using ledger-specific standard material price) may credit different amounts to the order in each ledger.

F3365 Display Document Flow- Order Confirmation GR

You can see the finance postings of material documents 5000000020 for both ledgers in the image below. Note that, in this case, different prices per ledger were used (keep in mind these values come from the released material cost estimate per ledger seen before)

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Amounts credited to the production order 0L

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Amounts credited to the production order 2L.

These values are shown as the preliminary production valuation (GR) in the Material Price Analysis app (see blue box in the attached image below):

CKM3N Preliminary valuation Goods Receipt 0L

CKM3N Preliminary valuation Goods Receipt 2L

9. Event-Based Settlement

Event-Based Settlement

 

SCOPE ITEM

2UG  3F0 Production Process by ledger event-based

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger-specific WIP and variance calculation and settlement.

UPA is only working with the event-based approach.

In the event-based approach (3F0), there are two models to trigger the settlement:

  • Settlement based on business events: Settlement is triggered automatically by business events such as goods movement or activity confirmation (RAK= RSEBW)
  • Manual Settlement: Settlement is initiated manually by setting the Order Settlement Trigger Type to Settled Manually instead of Based on Business Event (recommended for high volume scenarios). 

Please, check the configuration in your system.

During the first partial goods receipt, WIP starts to be reversed out proportionally until the final goods receipt or the CTEC status is reached. At that point, WIP will be completely cleared, and variances will be calculated and assigned to their respective variance categories.

Settlement based on business events provides real-time business insights and reduces period-end closing workload. Manual settlement helps decrease the large volume of settlement documents, especially in scenarios where backflush is heavily used and production orders have short lifecycles, making WIP analysis unnecessary.

Continuing with our example you can see ledger-specific debit and credit postings result in different WIP and variance amounts  calculated for each ledger and posted to the order.

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Variances 0L

The total production price difference for ledger 0L (307,75 EUR) is shown in the CKM3N App:

CKM3N Production Price Difference 0L.

The same logic applies for ledger 2L. In this case, the price difference is 513,01 EUR.

F4023 Display Line items- Cost Accounting Variances 2L

CKM3N Production Price Difference 2L

Note that event-based settlement generates one document per triggering event:

F4023 Display Line Items EBVP 0L

10 Preliminary COGS Values

Preliminary COGS Values

Preliminary values for goods issues associated with the sales order are derived from the released standard cost estimate per ledger explained before. Let’s track these types of movements in the Material Price Analysis app:

CKM3N Material Price Analysis 0L

Drilling down into the financial document posted with the goods issue for ledger 0L (two goods issues of 1 unit each)

F4023 Display Line Items 0L Goods Issue

Keep in mind that the COGS splitting document for these preliminary values is also created during the GI:

F4023 Display Line Items 0L Cost Splitting

And for ledger 2L:

CKM3N Material Price Analysis 2L

F4023 Display Line Items 2L Goods Issue

With the correspondent ledger specific COGS splitting:

F4023 Display Line Items 2L Cost Splitting

Verify that released material cost estimates per ledger are applied to stock movements across all currencies and parallel ledgers:

F1602 Manage Product Master Data

11 Actual Costing

Actual Costing

 

SCOPE ITEM

33Q Actual Costing

STEP (UPA-exclusive capabilities)

Ledger-specific actual material cost

Business Case Coverage

Ledger-specific contribution margin calculation, aligned with different accounting principles, is enabled since actual costing is performed per ledger and COGS are revaluated at the end of the period (PUP). This is an end-to-end process where full margin analysis can be reported by market segment and ledger, as costs are split by ledger across the entire value chain.

With UPA/Without UPA

Monitor Material prices App exclusive for UPA (App Id F7245)

Without UPA actual material inventory prices can only vary based on the valuation view (group/ profit center/ legal), and only one actual inventory price valuation is allowed in the legal view.

Without UPA APP Manage Material Valuations (App Id F2680) should be used instead.

Actual Price calculations are executed on a per-ledger basis: the Material Ledger derives actual prices and variance allocations separately for each ledger, so each ledger receives its own calculated actual price for materials. As a result, period-end ML close postings (inventory revaluation and COGS revaluation) are computed and posted per ledger, yielding different inventory values and cost of goods sold (COGS) amounts by ledger.

This closes the parallel valuation loop, enabling parallel FI postings and discrete contribution-margin reporting in Margin Analysis. In short, ledger-specific UPA plus Material Ledger close support a parallel value flow across end‑to‑end processes and let you analyse margins by ledger (see section 12. Profit Margins)

The different actual prices per ledger that are calculated for the legal valuation view can be analyzed and compared:

F7245 Monitor Material Prices- Actual Prices

Or analysed for all valuation types, where you can see differences between actual prices, inventory prices and standard prices (for example you can see that the actual price for ledger 0L is 25,46 EUR, while the standard price is 22,81 EUR)

F7245 Monitor Material Prices

Stock and GI (COGS) are updated by ledger with the corresponding adjustment and market segments assignments (product, sales order...) when actual costing run is performed, adjusting preliminary COGS values (See section 8. Preliminary COGS Values) and posting both the actual values for COGS and the ending inventory values (delta posting). Let’s take a look at the results for Ledger 0L:

CKM3N Material Price Analysis-Actual Costing 0L

Line-Items posted during actual costing run:

CKMLCP Actual Costing Run 0L

The adjustment for ending inventory is calculated as follows:

(actual price - standard price) * ending inventory quantity = (25,46€/Kg- 22,81 €/kg) * 198 Kg = 524,45 €

The adjustment for COGS is calculated as follows: 

(actual price - standard price) *GI from sales order quantity (period) = (25,46€/Kg- 22,81 €/kg) * 2Kg = 5,3 €

Let’s take a look at the results for Ledger 2L:

CKM3N Material Price Analysis-Actual Costing 2L

Line-Items posted during actual costing run:

CKMLCP Actual Costing Run 2L

12 Profit Margin

Profit Margin

To complete the E2E flow, let’s review how margins are affected depending on the ledger/accounting principle used for their calculation:

F0960 Market Segments- Actual Ledger 0L

Margin of 69.08 EUR: 120 EUR revenues minus 50.92 EUR Actual COGS (2uds * 25.46 EUR).

Actual COGS are calculated based on actual material costs. Note that the COGS split is retrieved from the actual costing cost components for ledger 0L  and a delta posting is created between actual and standard cost component splits from the preliminary COGS values (see Section 8. Preliminary COGS Values )

You can check it in the image below where the quantity is set to 2, which is the same as the sales quantity:

CKM3N Cost Components 0L

That comes from PUP calculated during actual costing run: 25,46 €/UN

CKM3N- Material Price Analysis Ledger 0L

Same logic applies to ledger 2L, with every specific ledger amounts:

F0960 Market Segments- Actual Ledger 2L

Margin of 51,92 EUR: 120 EUR revenues minus 68.08 EUR Actual COGS (2uds * 34.04 EUR).

CKM3N Cost Components 2L

CKM3N- Material Price Analysis Ledger 2L

Other App to analyze ledger-specific contribution margins:

W0182 Product profitability with production variances ledgers 0L and 2L

This brings us to the final step of the end-to-end process example.

Another Important part of the UPA implementation is the Best Practice Activation  

BEST PRACTICES ACTIVATION (AND DEACTIVATION) 

As you may know,  you can find SAP Best Practices in the SAP SIGNAVIO Process Navigator.

When Activating SAP Best Practices to Implement UPA, the first thing to be considered from a functional point of view,  is which UPA Best Practices needs to be activated and which ones (not valid for UPA) needs to be deactivated deactivated. Second thing to consider is required business functions. Please pay extra attention to SAP's standard documentation based on your system version. In fact UPA has its own Best Practices and request to deactivate some others. Furthermore, UPA has it's own post activation steps based on business scenarios you're going execute on system. You can check further details in the  Administration Guide

Please keep in mind that UPA comes with a set of UX mandates like usage of new Fiori apps instead of GUI transaction codes which makes Fiori usage a must for the system.

When setting up a Best Practice Client, specially in a multiple country activation,  one important step is to set up wisely the client currency (transaction SCC4). Also, how to setup that client also makes activation step more complicated or simple. We have chosen the "Alternative 1" to make it more simple. "Alternative 2" brings some pre-delivered configuration content from client "000" and some of them may be conflicting the ones delivered with Best Practice content.  Please check the Administration Guide for further details.

Some specific UPA best practices are related to the activation of the FINS_PARALLEL_ACCOUNTING_BF  business function. Please check the Administration Guide.

P.S. Regardless of UPA activation, please pay extra attention on activating required business functions and selecting best practices since there are some licensing implications.

To conclude, there are already many other blog posts written by the Product Owner and other SAP community members .

Universal Parallel Accounting in S/4HANA 

Overhead Accounting with Universal Parallel Accounting in S/4HANA 2025

Overhead Accounting with Universal Parallel Accounting S/4HANA 2022

Profit Center Valuation with Universal Parallel Accounting S/4HANA 2023

Inventory Accounting with Universal Parallel Accounting in S/4HANA 2022 

Please be aware that as stated by OSS 3191636 UPA can be activated via a Business Function. For details, please refer to the  Business Function Documentation

If you have more questions about UPA you can check the UPA FAQ contained in OSS  3265275;

If you want to start your journey with UPA it is suggested to apply for SAP Early adopter care program in UPA   

If you need to know how functionalities may evolve in future S/4HANA releases please have a look to the SAP Roadmap Explorer

Brought to you by a group of SAP Consultants in collaboration with the S/4HANA RIG 

Helena Carro @Helenacarro 

Mehmet Canca @mehmetcanca 

Vineek Gopan @Vineek 

Gianluca Taccone @gianluca_taccone 

Javier Trinidad  @Javier_Trinidad

entry.title: What is SAP plant reallocation?
entry.content:
If you’ve worked in SAP transformation for long enough, you quickly realise that organisational structures rarely stay static for very long. Companies acquire new businesses. They divest underperforming ones. Manufacturing footprints change. Supply chains get redesigned. Tax structures evolve. Geopolitical events suddenly make one region less attractive than another.

Over the past few years, those changes have arguably accelerated even further as organisations respond to inflation, supply chain disruption, rising labour costs and geopolitical uncertainty. In many cases, businesses are being forced to rethink where they manufacture products, where they hold inventory and how their operational footprint is structured.

This often creates a very specific SAP challenge. A business may decide to move manufacturing from one country to another, consolidate multiple production facilities into one location or transfer responsibility for a plant from one legal entity to another. From a business perspective, this may seem like a relatively straightforward organisational change. From an SAP perspective, however, it can be significantly more complicated.

Enter the SAP plant reallocation project.

What is SAP plant reallocation?

At a high level, SAP plant reallocation refers to moving an existing plant from one company code to another, following some kind of organisational restructuring. While this sounds relatively simple on paper, the challenge is that plants sit at the centre of many critical SAP processes and are heavily embedded within both logistics and finance functions.

A plant is typically linked to material master records, inventory, production orders, purchase orders, sales orders, valuation records, warehouse processes and financial postings. Over time, that plant builds up a significant amount of transactional history and operational dependencies. This means organisations cannot simply change a configuration setting and move on. They need a way to move the plant without breaking operational processes, losing historical data or creating major disruption for the business.

Why do organisations need plant reallocation?

The reasons for undertaking plant reallocations vary. In some cases, organisations are moving manufacturing operations to lower-cost regions to improve profitability. In others, businesses are redesigning supply chains to reduce dependency on specific geographies after experiencing disruptions in recent years.

Mergers and acquisitions can also create scenarios where newly acquired manufacturing locations need to be aligned to existing legal structures. Similarly, divestitures may require certain plants to move into new legal entities while others remain with the original business. Tax, regulatory and customs changes can also force businesses to rethink how their operational footprint is structured.

Regardless of the reason, the SAP challenge tends to remain the same: how do you move a plant from one company code to another without causing unnecessary operational disruption?

Why is this difficult in SAP?

This is where things become technically challenging. Once a plant has been active for a long period of time, it becomes connected to a wide range of master and transactional data. This can include stock, open purchase orders, open production orders, batch records, warehouse management data, valuation records, financial postings and reporting structures. Organisations also need to think carefully about historical reporting, audit requirements and financial continuity. Even if a plant moves to a new legal entity, the business still needs access to historical transactions and reporting data.

The traditional approach

Typically, many organisations have handled this challenge using a manual approach. This often involves freezing activity in the original plant, creating a new plant under the new company code and then manually recreating the relevant master data and transactional objects. Inventory may need to be transferred manually, open transactions may need to be recreated and finance teams often need to perform significant reconciliation work to ensure reporting remains accurate.

While this approach can work, it is time-consuming, it introduces operational downtime and creates significant manual effort. It can also introduce unnecessary risk, particularly when large volumes of transactions need to be recreated manually. And from the perspective of the business user, it creates a break between the transactional activity in the new company and the historical activity in the previous company code.

The table-based transformation approach

A more modern approach involves transforming the underlying SAP data model at table level rather than manually rebuilding everything from scratch. Instead of creating a completely new plant and migrating operational activity manually, organisations can use landscape transformation tools to update the relevant SAP tables so that the existing plant reflects the new organisational structure.

This approach typically involves updating plant assignments, company code relationships, valuation records and other dependent logistics and finance objects. Because these objects are often deeply interconnected, this requires a structured and controlled transformation process. This is where specialist tools such as Natuvion Data Conversion Suite (DCS) are often used.

Landscape transformation technology enables organisations to analyse the impacted data objects, identify dependencies between tables and execute the required transformation in a controlled way, at scale and at pace. Rather than manually rebuilding operational processes, the software helps organisations transform existing structures while preserving transactional history and reducing manual effort. You might think of this as business redesign from the inside out.

This can significantly reduce downtime, minimise disruption to the business and allow organisations to adapt far more quickly when operational changes are required.

Final thoughts

Plant reallocation in SAP is not something organisations should underestimate. Even with specialist transformation tooling, careful planning is required to understand which data objects are impacted, how integrations may be affected and what historical information needs to remain accessible after the move. Testing is also critical to ensure operational processes continue functioning as expected once the transformation has been completed.

Plant reallocation may not be discussed as frequently as carve-outs, cloud moves or S/4HANA migrations, but I suspect it will become increasingly common as businesses continue adapting their operational footprint in response to changing market conditions. As organisations become more agile, their SAP landscapes need to become more flexible as well. The traditional manual approach can still work, but table-based transformation approaches are increasingly offering organisations a quicker and less disruptive way to respond to change.

If you would like to read more on this topic, you can do so here.

entry.title: What is a SAP currency conversion?
entry.content:
If you read my previous article on SAP plant reallocation, you will have seen how political, economic and global change and uncertainty drive the requirement for complex technical change in SAP customers' ERP systems. 

Over the past few years, organisations have had to navigate inflation, volatile exchange rates, geopolitical instability and significant economic uncertainty. In some markets, businesses have faced sustained pressure from weakening local currencies. In others, governments have introduced entirely new currencies or announced plans to adopt new ones. Bulgaria’s recent adoption of the Euro is a good example of how these changes can quickly move from political discussion to operational reality.

When these events happen, businesses need to respond quickly. This often creates a very specific SAP challenge. How do you change the currency structure of an SAP system that may have been operating in the same currency for years?

At first glance, this may not sound particularly challenging. SAP already supports multiple currencies and most organisations regularly process transactions in foreign currencies. However, changing the company code currency is a very different challenge altogether. Once a company code has been operating for years, its currency becomes deeply embedded within financial processes, transactional records and reporting structures.

This is where a SAP currency conversion project comes in.

What is SAP currency conversion?

At a high level, SAP currency conversion refers to changing the local currency of a company code and converting the associated financial and transactional data so that the business can continue operating under a new currency structure.

This may happen because a country adopts a new national currency, because a business wants to move away from an unstable local currency or because organisational restructuring requires financial realignment across different currencies.

Regardless of the reason, the challenge tends to be the same. The business needs to move to a new currency structure. Your SAP ERP system, however, still contains years of financial history built around the old one. This means organisations cannot simply change a configuration setting and move on.

A company code currency is tied to general ledger balances, open receivables, open payables, asset accounting records, controlling objects and historical financial transactions. Over time, this creates a significant amount of dependency across the system. When the Eurozone countries moved to the Euro in 1999, the average SAP system would have been relatively small. Over the past quarter of a century, many have grown significantly. It's not unusual for SAP production systems to be 5TB and have billions of lines of transactional data. Some large ERP systems are ten times that size.

Why is this difficult in SAP?

If an organisation simply changes the currency setting at company code level, historical transactions remain stored in the original currency while new transactions begin posting in the new one. That quickly creates inconsistencies across financial reporting, reconciliation processes and audit requirements.

Businesses still need access to historical financial records and finance teams need confidence that reporting remains accurate before and after the conversion. Asset values need to remain consistent, open items need to be handled correctly and controlling structures need to continue functioning as expected. Integrations with downstream systems may also need to be reviewed.

Like many SAP transformation challenges, the configuration change itself is often relatively small. The real complexity sits within the underlying data.

The traditional approach

Historically, organisations often approached currency conversion through manual restructuring.

This might involve creating new company codes, migrating selected balances, manually adjusting financial records and maintaining historical environments for reporting purposes. Perhaps using application-level migration methods like BAPIs via a tool like LSMW,

While this approach can work, it often creates operational overheads. Finance teams may spend large amounts of time reconciling balances and manually validating financial outputs. And from the perspective of the business user, it creates a break between the transactional activity in the new company and the historical activity in the previous company code, requiring parallel reporting structures for at least a year or two.

The traditional approach often solves the immediate issue, but it is difficult and creates perhaps unnecessary complexity in the process. In some cases, organisations even delay the conversion because the perceived risk feels too high.

The table-based transformation approach

A more modern approach involves transforming the underlying SAP data model at table level rather than manually rebuilding financial structures from scratch.

Instead of creating entirely new company codes and manually recreating financial records, organisations can use specialist landscape transformation technology such as Natuvion DCS to convert the relevant SAP data directly.
